text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
We have now completed our study of the lampstand; Now we move to the next object on the Kohathites’ list. Numbers 4:11 says: “Over the golden altar they shall spread a cloth of blue and cover it with a covering of goatskin and shall put in its poles.” The golden altar. The altar of incense. This small but profoundly significant piece of furniture is the fourth item the Kohathites carried on their shoulders, and everything about it was chosen with care and purpose.

The Golden Altar of Incense is described in Exodus 30:1-6. It was a small square altar much smaller than the large Bronze Altar of Burnt Offering that stood in the outer court. Its measurements were one cubit long, one cubit wide, and two cubits high. It was a compact, upright altar, its position and its purpose gave it a significance far beyond its dimensions.

Like the Ark, the Table, and the Lampstand, it was made of acacia wood and overlaid with pure gold; the same combination throughout the Holy Place, wood and gold, the earthly covered in the divine. Around its top ran a gold rim or crown, and at each of its four corners extended projections called horns; also overlaid with gold. These horns were significant. The blood of the sin offering was applied to these horns by the high priest on the Day of Atonement. Under the moulding of the altar ran a gold border, and through two gold rings in the corner the carrying poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold were inserted so the Kohathites could lift it onto their shoulders and carry it through the wilderness.

The position of the Golden Altar sets it apart from every other piece of furniture in the tabernacle. Exodus 30:6 says Place the incense altar just outside the inner curtain that shields the Ark of the Covenant, in front of the Ark's cover; the place of atonement that covers the tablets inscribed with the terms of the covenant. I will meet with you there.” It stood directly in front of the veil; the thick curtain that separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place where the Ark of the Covenant and the Mercy Seat resided. Of all the furniture in the Holy Place, the Golden Altar stood the closest to the presence of God. The lampstand & the Showbread was further back. But the altar of incense stood immediately before the veil, as close to the Ark as anything could be without passing through the curtain.

This position was so significant that in 1 Kings 6:22 and Hebrews 9:3-4, the golden altar is spoken of in connection with the Most Holy Place, even though it physically stood in the Holy Place. It belonged to the innermost sanctuary in purpose and function, even when it stood in the outer room. It was the altar of the closest approach. It was placed at the threshold of the presence of God.

The sole purpose of the Golden Altar was the burning of incense. No sacrifice was ever made on it. No grain offering, no burnt offering, no blood offering. Exodus 30:9 specifically commanded: “You shall not offer unauthorized incense on it, or a burnt offering, or a grain offering, and you shall not pour a drink offering on it.” It was set apart exclusively for one thing: the burning of the holy incense before Yahweh. It was the most restricted piece of furniture in the tabernacle in terms of what was permitted on it.

The incense itself was a unique and sacred blend, described in Exodus 30:34-35 as a mixture of four specific spices; stacte, onycha, galbanum, and pure frankincense in equal portions, seasoned with salt, pure and holy. This exact formula was to be used only for the golden altar. Verse 37-38 adds a solemn warning: “You shall not make for yourselves any incense like it. It shall be for you holy to Yahweh. Whoever makes any like it to use as perfume shall be cut off from his people.” The incense formula belonged exclusively to Yahweh. It could not be copied, commercialised, or used for any ordinary purpose. What burned before the face of God was entirely set apart from everything else in the world.

The incense on the golden altar was burned twice every day without exception; every morning and every evening. Exodus 30:7-8 gives the exact instruction: “Aaron shall burn fragrant incense on it. When he tends the lamps in the morning, he shall burn it, and when Aaron sets up the lamps at twilight, he shall burn it, a regular incense offering before Yahweh throughout your generations.” Notice that the morning incense was burned at the same time as the lamps of the lampstand were tended in the morning, and the evening incense was burned at the same time as the lamps were lit in the evening.
The incense and the light were always tended together. The fragrance and the fire rose before Yahweh simultaneously, morning and evening, every single day.

Only Aaron and his sons; the priests - were permitted to burn incense on the golden altar. No ordinary Israelite could approach it. No Kohathite, though he carried the altar on his shoulders, could burn incense on it. The line between carrying and burning was absolute. Carrying required the Kohathites.
Burning required the priests. And the consequences of crossing that line were severe. Numbers 16 records the rebellion of Korah, a Kohathite, rebelled against Moses and Aaron. Along with him were 250 leaders of Israel, who took censers and attempted to offer incense before Yahweh without priestly authority.
Fire came out from Yahweh and consumed the 250 men who offered incense. The lesson was unmistakable: the right to approach God in incense was not a right that could be claimed by human ambition or assumed by position. It was given by God alone, to those He appointed.

Once a year, on the Day of Atonement, the high priest performed a unique additional act at the golden altar. He took coals from the altar’s fire, filled a censer with the sacred incense, and carried both behind the veil into the Most Holy Place itself. The cloud of incense would fill the Most Holy Place and cover the Mercy Seat, so that the high priest would not die in the presence of Yahweh (Leviticus 16:12-13). On this one day each year, the incense that normally burned before the veil was carried through the veil. The aroma that rose before God every morning and evening entered God’s presence directly on the holiest day of the year.

When the camp prepared to move and the Kohathites came to carry the golden altar, the priests followed a specific order of covering. First, a cloth of blue was spread over the altar. Then over the blue cloth was laid a covering of sea-cow skin or badger skin the same tough, durable, waterproof outer leather that covered the Ark and the Lampstand during transport. Then the carrying poles were inserted through the gold rings and the altar was lifted onto the shoulders of the Kohathites. The blue cloth beneath the leather was hidden from the outside. The gold beneath the blue was hidden further still. What the carrier felt on his shoulder was leather. What the world saw passing through the wilderness was a bundle of skin. But inside the layers was the gold altar on which the fragrance of God’s people had risen before Yahweh every morning and every evening.

We have now seen what the Golden Altar was; its size, its materials, its position before the veil, its exclusive purpose, its daily use morning and evening, and who was permitted to burn upon it. But there is a question the altar is waiting to answer. Why did this specific altar matter so much that God placed it closest to the veil? Why was its formula unique and uncopyable? Why did incense have to rise twice every day without fail? And what does all of this; the gold, the fragrance, the position before the face of God, the morning and evening offering point to in Christ? That is what we will study tomorrow. The altar is prepared. The incense is ready. The fragrance is already beginning to rise.

title: Prayer
text:
Let my prayer be counted as incense before You, Yahweh, the lifting up of my hands as the evening sacrifice (Psalm 141:2). You are the God who desires the fragrance of Your people rising before Your face; not occasionally, not only when circumstances are favourable, but every morning and every evening, without a single day of absence. You set the golden altar closest to the veil, closest to Your presence, because prayer was the ministry You placed nearest to Your own heart. We thank You for this altar. We thank You that You did not leave Your people without a way to approach You, without a place where the fragrance of their worship could rise before Your face. As we study this altar in the days ahead, teach us to understand what it truly means to draw near.
And forgive us for the mornings and evenings when the altar in our own lives has gone cold; when the incense has not been burned and the fragrance has not risen. Tend the altar in us. In Christ’s name, amen.
